quizBank.question: What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?
quizBank.answer: PUT replaces the entire resource. If you omit fields, they are deleted or set to default. PATCH applies a partial update — only the fields you send are modified. Example: PATCH /users/123 with {name: 'Alice'} updates only the name field; PUT /users/123 with {name: 'Alice'} would clear all other fields not included. In practice: use PATCH for partial updates (more common), PUT when you always send the full representation.

quizBank.question: When would you choose gRPC over REST?
quizBank.answer: Choose gRPC when: (1) Internal service-to-service communication where you control both client and server. (2) High-throughput scenarios — Protobuf is 3-10x smaller than JSON, HTTP/2 multiplexing reduces connection overhead. (3) Strongly typed contracts are important. (4) Streaming is needed (server streaming, client streaming, bidirectional). (5) Polyglot environments — generated clients for many languages. Choose REST when: public API, browser support needed, teams are unfamiliar with gRPC.

quizBank.question: Explain the token bucket rate limiting algorithm.
quizBank.answer: A token bucket has a maximum capacity of N tokens. Tokens are added at a constant rate R per second (up to capacity). Each request consumes one token. If the bucket has tokens, the request is allowed and a token is consumed. If empty, the request is rejected (429 Too Many Requests). This allows bursting — if a user has been idle, they accumulate tokens and can burst. The refill rate controls average throughput; the capacity controls burst size. In Redis: store (tokens, last_refill_time) per user; use a Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement.

quizBank.question: Why is cursor pagination preferred over offset pagination for large datasets?
quizBank.answer: Offset pagination (LIMIT 20 OFFSET 10000) has two problems: (1) Performance: database must scan and discard 10,000 rows to return 20. With large offsets, this is very slow — O(n). (2) Consistency: if items are inserted or deleted between page fetches, items can appear twice or be skipped. Cursor pagination uses a stable reference point (e.g., last seen ID or timestamp). The query becomes WHERE id > cursor LIMIT 20, which is an indexed seek — O(log n). It is consistent because new inserts do not affect the cursor position.

quizBank.question: What is an idempotency key and why does the Stripe API use it?
quizBank.answer: An idempotency key is a client-generated unique identifier sent with a request. If the same key is seen again, the server returns the cached response from the first request instead of processing again. For payment APIs: if a network timeout occurs after the server processed a charge but before the client received the response, the client doesn't know if the charge succeeded. Retrying without idempotency would double-charge. With an idempotency key, retrying safely returns the original result. Stripe stores (key -> response) for 24 hours.

quizBank.question: What are the main differences between REST and GraphQL?
quizBank.answer: REST: Multiple endpoints (one per resource), returns fixed data shape. Over-fetching (too much data) and under-fetching (multiple requests needed) are common. HTTP caching works naturally. GraphQL: Single endpoint, client specifies exactly which fields it needs (no over/under-fetching). Strong type system with schema introspection. Hard to cache (queries are POST, field-level caching requires persisted queries). Adds N+1 problem on the server side (use DataLoader). Good for: complex data graphs, mobile apps, rapid frontend iteration. REST good for: public APIs, simple resources.

quizBank.question: What HTTP status code should you return when rate limiting and what headers should accompany it?
quizBank.answer: Return 429 Too Many Requests. Headers to include: X-RateLimit-Limit (total allowed in window), X-RateLimit-Remaining (remaining in current window), X-RateLimit-Reset (Unix timestamp when limit resets), Retry-After (seconds to wait before retrying). Response body should include a human-readable message and link to rate limiting docs. Clients should always check for 429 and implement exponential backoff rather than immediate retry.

quizBank.question: How do you design an API that is backward compatible as it evolves?
quizBank.answer: Rules for non-breaking changes: (1) Add new optional fields to responses — existing clients ignore unknown fields. (2) Add new optional request fields with sensible defaults. (3) Add new endpoints. Breaking changes (require version bump): removing or renaming fields, changing types, removing endpoints, changing semantics. Strategy: use URL versioning (/v1/, /v2/), maintain old versions for 6-12 months after deprecation announcement, use OpenAPI to document changes, consumer-driven contract testing (Pact) to catch breaks before deployment.

quizBank.question: How do you secure a webhook endpoint on the receiving side?
quizBank.answer: Webhook security: (1) Signature verification — sender signs payload with HMAC-SHA256 using a shared secret; receiver recomputes and compares signatures. (2) Replay attack prevention — include a timestamp in the payload; reject if timestamp is older than 5 minutes. (3) IP allowlisting — only accept from known sender IPs (fragile if sender uses CDN). (4) Use HTTPS only. (5) Return 200 quickly and process asynchronously to avoid timeouts causing retries. (6) Idempotent processing — webhooks may be delivered multiple times.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
quizBank.answer: Authentication (AuthN): Verifying identity — who are you? Methods: password, API key, JWT, OAuth token, certificate. Authorization (AuthZ): Determining permissions — what can you do? Methods: RBAC (role-based), ABAC (attribute-based), ACL. HTTP status codes: 401 Unauthorized means not authenticated (despite the name). 403 Forbidden means authenticated but not authorized. Always authenticate first, then authorize.

quizBank.question: How would you design an API rate limiter that works across multiple servers?
quizBank.answer: Local rate limiters do not work across multiple application servers (each has its own counter). Distributed rate limiting: (1) Redis centralized counter — all servers atomically increment a shared Redis counter per API key per time window. Use INCR with EXPIRE for fixed windows, or Lua scripts for atomic token bucket. (2) Redis token bucket — store (tokens, timestamp) in Redis, Lua script to atomically refill and consume. (3) External rate limiting service (Kong, AWS API Gateway) — handles rate limiting before requests reach your servers. Trade-off: adds Redis round trip to every request (~1ms).

quizBank.question: What are the OAuth 2.0 grant types and when do you use each?
quizBank.answer: Authorization Code + PKCE: User-facing web/mobile apps. User redirects to auth server, gets authorization code, exchanges for access token. Most secure — tokens never in URL. Client Credentials: Service-to-service, no user context. Service authenticates with client ID + secret, gets access token. Implicit (deprecated): Old browser apps, token returned directly in URL fragment. Avoid. Device Authorization: TVs, IoT, CLI tools without browsers. Device shows a code; user authenticates on phone/computer. Resource Owner Password Credentials (deprecated): Direct username/password. Avoid except for first-party apps.

cheatSheet:
# Distributed Systems

## CAP Theorem

In a distributed system during a network partition, you must choose between Consistency and Availability.

- **Consistency**: Every read receives the most recent write or an error
- **Availability**: Every request receives a response (but may be stale)
- **Partition Tolerance**: System continues operating despite network partitions

Since P is mandatory in real distributed systems, the real choice is C vs A:
- **CP**: HBase, ZooKeeper, etcd, MongoDB (majority write concern)
- **AP**: Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB (default)

## PACELC Theorem

Extends CAP: Even without partitions, you must choose between Latency (L) and Consistency (C).

```
If Partition: choose Availability or Consistency
Else (no partition): choose Latency or Consistency

Cassandra: PA/EL — AP during partition; low latency over consistency normally
HBase: PC/EC — CP during partition; consistency over latency
DynamoDB: PA/EL — eventual consistency default; strong consistency at higher latency
```

## Consistency Models (Weakest to Strongest)

1. **Eventual Consistency**: All replicas converge eventually. Reads may be stale. (Cassandra, DNS)
2. **Monotonic Read Consistency**: Once you read a value, you never read an older value.
3. **Read-Your-Writes**: After a write, your own reads see it immediately.
4. **Session Consistency**: Monotonic reads + read-your-writes within a session.
5. **Causal Consistency**: Causally related operations seen in causal order by all.
6. **Linearizability**: Operations appear instantaneous; once a write commits, all reads see it. (Strongest, highest latency)

## Raft Consensus Algorithm

```mermaid
graph LR
    L[Leader] -->|Heartbeat| F1[Follower 1]
    L -->|Heartbeat| F2[Follower 2]
    L -->|AppendEntries| F1
    L -->|AppendEntries| F2
    F1 -->|Vote| C{Candidate on timeout}
    F2 -->|Vote| C
    C -->|Wins majority| L2[New Leader]
```

**Leader Election**: All nodes start as followers. If no heartbeat within election timeout, become candidate. First to get majority (N/2+1) votes becomes leader.

**Log Replication**: Leader appends client writes to log, replicates to followers. Once majority acknowledge, marks as committed.

## Consistent Hashing

```python
import hashlib, bisect

class ConsistentHashRing:
    def __init__(self, virtual_nodes=150):
        self.ring = {}
        self.sorted_keys = []
        self.virtual_nodes = virtual_nodes
    
    def add_node(self, node):
        for i in range(self.virtual_nodes):
            key = self._hash(f'{node}:{i}')
            self.ring[key] = node
            bisect.insort(self.sorted_keys, key)
    
    def get_node(self, key):
        hash_key = self._hash(key)
        idx = bisect.bisect(self.sorted_keys, hash_key) % len(self.sorted_keys)
        return self.ring[self.sorted_keys[idx]]
    
    def _hash(self, key):
        return int(hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
```

Virtual nodes ensure even distribution even with heterogeneous nodes.

## Vector Clocks

Track causality in distributed systems.

```
Node A: [A:1, B:0] sends message to B
Node B: receives, updates: [A:1, B:1]
Compare: V1 < V2 if every component of V1 <= V2 and at least one strictly <
Concurrent if neither dominates — conflict detected
```

Used by Amazon Dynamo, Riak for conflict detection.

## Two-Phase Commit (2PC)

```
Phase 1 (Prepare): Coordinator asks all participants: Can you commit?
Phase 2 (Commit): If all Yes — Commit. If any No — Abort.
```

**Problem**: Blocking — if coordinator crashes after participants voted Yes but before sending Commit, participants hold locks indefinitely. Replaced by Saga pattern in modern systems.

## Distributed Locking (Redlock)

```python
# Acquire lock on majority of N Redis nodes
def acquire_redlock(resource, ttl_ms):
    random_value = generate_random()
    acquired = 0
    start = time_ms()
    for redis_node in redis_nodes:
        if redis_node.set(resource, random_value, nx=True, px=ttl_ms):
            acquired += 1
    elapsed = time_ms() - start
    if acquired >= len(redis_nodes) // 2 + 1 and elapsed < ttl_ms:
        return Lock(random_value)  # Success
    release_all_locks(random_value)
    return None  # Failed
```

## Gossip Protocol

Each node periodically selects random peers and exchanges state. Information spreads in O(log N) rounds.

```
Round 1: Node A tells B -> 2 nodes know
Round 2: A tells C, B tells D -> 4 nodes know
Round 3: A,B,C,D each tell someone -> ~8 nodes know
```

Used by Cassandra, Consul for failure detection and membership.

## CRDTs

Data structures that can be updated concurrently and merged without conflicts.
- **G-Counter**: Increment-only; value = sum across all replicas
- **LWW-Register**: Last-Write-Wins using timestamps
- **OR-Set**: Add/remove set where add wins concurrent conflicts

## Merkle Trees

Tree of hashes used to detect inconsistencies between replicas in O(log N) instead of O(N). Used by DynamoDB, Cassandra for anti-entropy repair, Git for object storage.

## FLP Impossibility

In a purely asynchronous distributed system, consensus is impossible if even one node can fail. Practical systems use timeouts (partial synchrony) to work around this.

quizBank.question: Explain CAP theorem with a practical example.
quizBank.answer: CAP theorem: A distributed system can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. Since network partitions are inevitable, the choice is C vs A. Example: A shopping cart during a network partition between two data centers. Consistent choice (CP) — reject writes in the isolated partition; users see an error but data is always accurate. Available choice (AP) — accept writes in both partitions; after partition heals, merge conflicting carts (Amazon's Dynamo does this). Amazon chose AP for shopping carts because an empty cart error is a worse user experience than a slightly inconsistent cart.

quizBank.question: How does Raft ensure only one leader exists at a time?
quizBank.answer: Raft uses monotonically increasing terms. When a candidate requests votes, it includes its term. A candidate must receive votes from a majority (N/2+1) to become leader — this is impossible for two nodes simultaneously since a majority is strictly greater than half. If a server receives a message with a higher term, it updates its term and reverts to follower. If two candidates tie, neither gets majority; they wait for a random election timeout and try again. The combination of terms and majority voting ensures at most one leader per term.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between linearizability and eventual consistency?
quizBank.answer: Linearizability (strong consistency): Every operation appears to take effect instantaneously at some point between its invocation and completion. Once a write is acknowledged, all subsequent reads from any client see that write. Strongest guarantee; highest latency. Eventual consistency: Given no new updates, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. Reads may return stale data indefinitely. Lowest latency; highest availability. Most practical systems use an intermediate model: causal consistency, read-your-writes, or monotonic reads.

quizBank.question: What is consistent hashing and how do virtual nodes improve it?
quizBank.answer: Consistent hashing places nodes on a virtual ring. A key is assigned to the first node clockwise from the key's hash. When a node is added or removed, only keys between it and its predecessor are remapped (~K/N keys vs nearly all keys in modular hashing). Virtual nodes: each physical node has multiple positions on the ring (e.g., 150). Benefits: (1) More even distribution with few nodes. (2) Heterogeneous nodes — high-capacity nodes get more virtual nodes. (3) Smoother redistribution when nodes join or leave. Used by Cassandra, DynamoDB, Memcached.

quizBank.question: What problems does 2PC have and what replaces it?
quizBank.answer: 2PC problems: (1) Blocking — if the coordinator crashes after Phase 1 (participants voted Yes) but before Phase 2, participants hold locks indefinitely waiting for the coordinator. (2) Coordinator is a SPOF. (3) Does not tolerate partitions well. (4) High latency — requires two round trips. Alternatives: (1) Saga pattern — break distributed transaction into local transactions with compensating transactions for rollback. No distributed locking. (2) Optimistic concurrency control. (3) Avoid distributed transactions by redesigning service boundaries.

quizBank.question: What are vector clocks and what problem do they solve?
quizBank.answer: Vector clocks solve the problem of determining causality between events in a distributed system where physical clocks cannot be trusted. A vector clock is a vector of counters, one per node. Rules: When a node processes an event, increment its own counter. When sending a message, attach the current vector. When receiving, take the element-wise maximum of received and own vectors, then increment own counter. Comparison: V1 happened-before V2 if every element of V1 is <= V2 and at least one is strictly less. If neither dominates, events are concurrent — conflict detected. Used by Dynamo for conflict detection.

quizBank.question: How does gossip protocol achieve failure detection?
quizBank.answer: In gossip-based failure detection (Cassandra, Consul): each node maintains a heartbeat counter that it increments periodically. Nodes exchange their state tables with random peers. If a node has not heard from another node for a threshold time, it suspects that node is down. After a longer timeout with no heartbeats, it declares the node dead. Gossip spreads failure information in O(log N) rounds. Advantages: decentralized (no SPOF), scalable, eventually consistent failure detection. Cassandra uses the phi accrual failure detector, which adjusts suspicion threshold based on observed network conditions.

quizBank.question: What is split-brain and how do systems handle it?
quizBank.answer: Split-brain occurs when a network partition causes a cluster to split into two groups, each believing the other is dead and both becoming active (e.g., two primary databases). Problems: conflicting writes in both partitions, data divergence. Prevention: (1) Quorum/majority requirement — a leader must maintain communication with majority (N/2+1). If it cannot, it steps down. Only one partition can have a majority. (2) Fencing tokens — distributed lock includes a monotonically increasing token; old leader's writes are rejected by the storage layer. (3) STONITH — fencing mechanism that kills the isolated node.

quizBank.question: What is PACELC and how does it extend CAP?
quizBank.answer: PACELC: If Partition, choose Availability or Consistency; Else, choose Latency or Consistency. CAP only addresses behavior during partitions. PACELC captures the latency vs consistency trade-off during normal operation. Cassandra is PA/EL — during partitions it chooses availability; during normal operation it favors low latency over strong consistency. HBase is PC/EC — during partitions it becomes unavailable to stay consistent; during normal operation it favors consistency over latency. This is more practical than CAP because high latency hurts users even without partitions.

quizBank.question: What are CRDTs and when would you use them?
quizBank.answer: CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) are data structures designed to be updated concurrently on multiple replicas and merged without conflicts — merging is commutative, associative, and idempotent. Examples: G-Counter (increment-only), PN-Counter (increment and decrement), LWW-Register (Last Write Wins), OR-Set (add and remove). Use CRDTs when: you need high availability and can model your data as a CRDT, collaborative editing, shopping carts, distributed counters. Limitation: not all data models can be expressed as CRDTs.

quizBank.question: How do Merkle trees help with data consistency across replicas?
quizBank.answer: Merkle trees provide an efficient way to detect and locate inconsistencies between replicas without transferring all data. Each leaf contains the hash of a data block. Each internal node contains the hash of its children. Comparing two replicas: (1) Compare root hashes — if equal, replicas are identical. (2) If different, recursively compare children to locate which subtree differs. (3) Sync only the differing leaf nodes. This is O(log N) for locating differences vs O(N) for full comparison. Used in DynamoDB and Cassandra for anti-entropy repair, Git for efficient object storage.

quizBank.question: What is the FLP impossibility result and what are its practical implications?
quizBank.answer: FLP (Fischer, Lynch, Paterson 1985) proved that in a purely asynchronous distributed system where message delivery delays are unbounded, it is impossible to achieve consensus if even one process can fail. Practical implications: (1) Real consensus algorithms (Raft, Paxos) use timeouts — converting asynchronous to partially synchronous. (2) Consensus algorithms can guarantee safety (never choose wrong value) but not liveness (may not terminate) in all cases. (3) Google Spanner uses TrueTime (atomic clocks + GPS) to bound clock uncertainty and provide near-synchronous guarantees.

cheatSheet:
# Scalability

## Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

| Dimension | Vertical (Scale Up) | Horizontal (Scale Out) |
|---|---|---|
| How | Bigger server (more CPU/RAM) | More servers of same size |
| Limit | Physical hardware limit | Near-unlimited |
| Cost | Expensive at top end, no redundancy | Commodity hardware, built-in redundancy |
| Complexity | Simple | Requires distributed systems thinking |
| Failure | Single point of failure | Fault tolerant |
| When to use | First step, databases | Stateless services, large scale |

## Stateless Services

```python
# Stateful (bad)
app.session['cart'] = [item1, item2]  # Stored on this specific server

# Stateless (good)
cart = redis.get(f'cart:{user_id}')   # State in Redis, any server can handle request
```

Stateless checklist: Sessions in Redis, file uploads to S3, config from env vars, logs to stdout.

## Database Scaling Journey

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Single DB] -->|Read-heavy| B[Add Read Replicas]
    B -->|Write-heavy| C[Vertical Scale DB]
    C -->|Still bottlenecked| D[Add Caching Layer]
    D -->|Still bottlenecked| E[Application-level sharding]
    E -->|Specific workloads| F[Purpose-built DBs]
```

## Connection Pooling at Scale

100 app servers x 20 connections = 2,000 DB connections. PostgreSQL each connection ~10MB RAM = 20GB just for connections. PgBouncer in transaction mode: 2,000 app connections -> 50 actual DB connections.

## Queue-Based Load Leveling

```
Without queue: 10,000 req/sec spike -> DB overwhelmed -> outage
With queue: 10,000 req/sec -> Queue (SQS/Kafka) -> 1,000 workers at steady pace -> DB stable
```

## Auto-Scaling

```yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
spec:
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 50
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: External
    external:
      metric:
        name: sqs_queue_depth
      target:
        type: AverageValue
        averageValue: 100
```

## Sharding

```python
def get_shard(user_id, num_shards=10):
    return hash(user_id) % num_shards

def get_db(user_id):
    shard = get_shard(user_id)
    return db_connections[shard]
```

Challenges: cross-shard joins (scatter-gather or denormalize), resharding (consistent hashing minimizes remapping), hot shards (compound key or dedicated shard).

## CQRS

```
Write: POST /orders -> PostgreSQL (normalized)
       -> Kafka (order.created event)
       -> Projection service -> Elasticsearch (denormalized)

Read: GET /orders/search?q=pizza -> Elasticsearch directly
```

## Multi-Region Active-Active

```mermaid
graph TB
    DNS[GeoDNS] --> US[US-East]
    DNS --> EU[EU-West]
    DNS --> AP[AP-Southeast]
    US <-->|Async Replication| EU
    EU <-->|Async Replication| AP
    AP <-->|Async Replication| US
```

Challenges: conflict resolution (last-write-wins, CRDTs), eventual consistency across regions.

## C10K Problem

Thread-per-connection: 10K threads x 2MB stack = 20GB RAM. Solutions: event loop (Nginx, Node.js), epoll/kqueue at OS level, Go goroutines (~4KB stack).

## Estimation Framework

```
Write QPS = DAU x writes_per_user / 86400
Read QPS  = DAU x reads_per_user / 86400
Peak QPS  = Average QPS x 3
Storage   = writes_per_day x bytes_per_write x retention_days
Servers   = Peak_QPS / QPS_per_server (typical: 1000-5000 QPS)
```

## Key Principles

1. Measure first — profile before optimizing
2. Scale databases last — exhaust caching and query optimization first
3. Stateless services scale trivially
4. Async > sync — queues decouple and absorb spikes
5. Fail fast — timeouts and circuit breakers prevent cascades

quizBank.question: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling, and when do you prefer each?
quizBank.answer: Vertical scaling (scale up) adds more resources (CPU, RAM, disk) to a single server. Simple, no code changes, but has a hard limit and is a SPOF. Horizontal scaling (scale out) adds more servers. Unlimited scale, built-in redundancy, but requires stateless services and distributed system complexity. Prefer vertical first (simpler), then horizontal when vertical limits are reached. Databases are often vertically scaled longer because horizontal sharding is complex. Stateless web and application servers should be horizontally scaled for availability.

quizBank.question: Why must services be stateless for horizontal scaling to work?
quizBank.answer: If a service stores state locally (in-memory sessions, local files), subsequent requests from the same client must go to the same server (sticky sessions). This breaks even load distribution and complicates failover. Stateless services store state externally (Redis for sessions, S3 for files, DB for data). Any server can handle any request, load balancing works perfectly, and any server can die without losing client state. This is the prerequisite for unlimited horizontal scaling.

quizBank.question: What is CQRS and what scalability benefit does it provide?
quizBank.answer: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) uses separate models for writes (commands) and reads (queries). Write side: normalized, ACID-compliant database optimized for writes. Read side: denormalized, read-optimized store (Elasticsearch, Cassandra, Redis) optimized for specific query patterns. Scalability benefits: (1) Read and write sides scale independently. (2) Read models can be tailored to specific queries with no complex joins. (3) Read replicas or separate read stores handle 99% of traffic. Trade-off: eventual consistency — reads may lag behind writes.

quizBank.question: How do you handle database hot spots in a sharded system?
quizBank.answer: Hot spots occur when a sharding key concentrates traffic on one shard (e.g., all writes for a popular user). Solutions: (1) Compound key — append a random suffix to distribute writes: shard by user_id + random(0,10), aggregate on read. (2) Consistent hashing with virtual nodes — more ring positions for the hot shard. (3) Dedicated shard — move the hot key (celebrity) to its own shard. (4) Caching — cache hot keys to reduce shard load. (5) Application-level fan-out — for celebrity writes, fan out to N caches instead of hitting DB.

quizBank.question: What is queue-based load leveling and when would you use it?
quizBank.answer: Queue-based load leveling puts a message queue between producer (web server) and consumer (worker service). The queue absorbs traffic spikes — producers write at burst rate, consumers process at a steady sustainable rate. Use when: (1) Bursty traffic patterns (flash sales, batch jobs). (2) Processing takes time (image resize, email, ML inference). (3) Downstream service has limited capacity. (4) You want to decouple production from processing. Monitor queue depth to determine whether consumers need scaling.

quizBank.question: How does the C10K problem relate to modern web server design?
quizBank.answer: The C10K problem (10,000 concurrent connections, Dan Kegel 1999): traditional thread-per-connection servers (Apache prefork) require 10K threads x 2MB stack = 20GB RAM, plus context switching overhead. Solutions: (1) Event-driven I/O — Nginx uses a single-threaded event loop with non-blocking I/O via epoll/kqueue. One thread handles thousands of connections. (2) Node.js — single-threaded event loop for I/O-bound workloads. (3) Go goroutines — lightweight (~4KB stack), multiplexed onto OS threads. Modern systems routinely handle 1M+ concurrent connections on a single machine.

quizBank.question: Explain the scaling journey for a database from 1,000 to 100 million users.
quizBank.answer: 1K-10K users: single DB, optimize queries, add indexes. 10K-100K: add read replicas for read offloading, add Redis caching. 100K-1M: vertical scale DB (bigger instance), tune connection pool (PgBouncer), partition tables by date. 1M-10M: application-level caching, CQRS (separate read store like Elasticsearch), move analytics to data warehouse. 10M-100M: application-level sharding (shard by user_id or tenant_id), move hot workloads to purpose-built databases (Cassandra for writes, Redis for counters). At each step: measure, find the bottleneck, solve only that.

quizBank.question: What is auto-scaling and what are the challenges in implementing it?
quizBank.answer: Auto-scaling automatically adjusts the number of compute instances based on load. Challenges: (1) Warm-up time — new instances take time to start and be ready (JVM warm-up, connection pool establishment), causing latency spikes during scale-out. (2) Scale-in risk — terminating instances while they have active connections. Use connection draining. (3) Thrashing — oscillating between scale-out and scale-in. Use cooldown periods. (4) Stateful services cannot be auto-scaled without external state management. (5) Cost — reactive scaling is wasteful; predictive scaling is better but complex.

quizBank.question: How do you design a system for multi-region active-active deployment?
quizBank.answer: Active-active multi-region: each region handles both reads and writes. Key challenges: (1) Data replication: async replication between regions (50-200ms latency) means eventual consistency across regions. (2) Conflict resolution: if users in US-East and EU-West write the same record simultaneously. Strategies: last-write-wins (timestamp), CRDTs, application-level merge. (3) Routing: GeoDNS or Anycast routes users to nearest region. (4) Global services (auth, billing) may need strong consistency — use a single CP system carefully. (5) Operational complexity across regions.

quizBank.question: How do you estimate capacity for a system handling 100M DAU?
quizBank.answer: Framework: DAU x actions_per_user_per_day / 86400 = average QPS. Peak = average x 3. Storage = writes_per_day x record_size x retention. Example for Twitter-like feed: 300M DAU, 5 reads/user/day + 0.1 write/user/day. Read QPS: 300M x 5 / 86400 = ~17,000 QPS. Write QPS: 300M x 0.1 / 86400 = ~350 QPS. Peak read: ~50,000 QPS. With 10K QPS per server: need ~5 read servers (more with replication). Storage: 350 QPS x 500 bytes x 86400s x 365 days = ~5.5 TB/year.

quizBank.question: What is connection pooling and why is it critical at scale?
quizBank.answer: Each database connection is expensive: PostgreSQL creates an OS process per connection (~5-10MB RAM, ~50ms setup time). At scale: 100 app servers x 20 connections = 2,000 connections -> 10-20GB RAM just for connections. Connection poolers (PgBouncer, HikariCP) maintain a small pool of actual DB connections and multiplex many application connections onto them. PgBouncer transaction mode: 2,000 app connections -> 50 actual DB connections. This allows scaling app servers without proportionally scaling DB connections. Critical for PostgreSQL-based systems at scale.

quizBank.question: How does Kubernetes HPA work and what metrics can trigger scaling?
quizBank.answer: Kubernetes HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) automatically scales pod replicas based on observed metrics. Desired replicas = ceil(current_replicas x (current_metric / target_metric)). Metrics: (1) CPU utilization (most common) — scale when average CPU > 70%. (2) Memory — less common (memory leaks cause false positives). (3) Custom metrics via Metrics API — queue depth (scale workers when SQS queue > 100 messages), request latency (scale when p99 > 500ms). (4) External metrics from external systems. Best practice: use CPU for latency-sensitive services; use queue depth for worker pools. Set minReplicas > 1 for availability.

cheatSheet:
# System Design Case Studies

## Interview Framework

```
1. Clarify requirements (5 min)
   - Functional: what features?
   - Non-functional: scale, latency, consistency, availability
   - Constraints: read-heavy vs write-heavy, DAU, QPS, storage

2. Estimate scale (5 min)
   - DAU, QPS (read/write), storage, bandwidth

3. High-level design (10 min)
   - APIs, data model, core components

4. Deep dive (20 min)
   - Bottlenecks, trade-offs, failure scenarios

5. Wrap-up (5 min)
   - Monitoring, scaling, future improvements
```

## URL Shortener (bit.ly)

**Requirements**: Shorten URLs, redirect, 100M URLs/day writes, 10:1 read/write ratio.

**Estimation**:
- Write QPS: 100M / 86400 = ~1,160/sec
- Read QPS: ~11,600/sec
- Storage: 100M/day x 500 bytes x 5 years = ~90 TB

**Short code generation**:
```python
# Option 1: Hash-based (MD5/SHA256, take first 7 chars)
import hashlib
def shorten(long_url):
    return hashlib.md5(long_url.encode()).hexdigest()[:7]
# Problem: hash collisions

# Option 2: Base62 encoding of unique ID (preferred)
import string
BASE62 = string.ascii_letters + string.digits  # 62 chars

def encode_base62(num):
    result = []
    while num > 0:
        result.append(BASE62[num % 62])
        num //= 62
    return ''.join(reversed(result))

# ID generation: distributed counter (Snowflake ID) or DB auto-increment
# 7 chars in base62 = 62^7 = 3.5 trillion URLs
```

**301 vs 302 redirect**:
- 301 (Permanent): Browser caches redirect. Reduces server load. Use when you don't need analytics per-visit.
- 302 (Temporary): Browser re-requests every time. Server sees each request. Use for analytics tracking.

**Architecture**:
```mermaid
graph LR
    U[User] --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> WS[Web Servers]
    WS --> Cache[Redis Cache]
    WS --> DB[(Database\nCassandra / MySQL)]
    Cache -->|Cache miss| DB
```

Cassandra schema:
```sql
CREATE TABLE urls (
    short_code TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    long_url    TEXT,
    user_id     UUID,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP
);
```

## Chat System (WhatsApp/Slack)

**Requirements**: 1:1 and group messaging, message persistence, online/offline status, 500M DAU.

**Key decisions**:
- **WebSocket** for real-time message delivery (persistent connection per client)
- **Message storage**: Cassandra (high write throughput, time-ordered)
- **Message ID**: Snowflake (time-sortable, unique across nodes)

**Cassandra schema**:
```sql
CREATE TABLE messages (
    conversation_id UUID,
    message_id      BIGINT,     -- Snowflake ID (time-ordered)
    sender_id       UUID,
    content         TEXT,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (conversation_id, message_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC);
```

**Online presence**:
- Client sends heartbeat every 5 seconds
- If no heartbeat for 10 seconds, mark offline
- Store status in Redis (low latency reads)

**Message delivery flow**:
```
Sender -> Chat Server A -> Message Queue (Kafka)
Message Queue -> Chat Server B (recipient's server) -> WebSocket -> Recipient
Message Queue -> Storage Service -> Cassandra (persist)
```

## News Feed (Twitter/Instagram)

**Two approaches**:

### Fan-out on Write (Push)
- When user posts, write to all followers' feed caches immediately
- Read is O(1) — just read pre-computed feed
- Problem: Celebrity problem (Justin Bieber has 100M followers — 100M writes per tweet)

### Fan-out on Read (Pull)
- Feed is computed at read time by fetching posts from followed users
- No write amplification
- Problem: Read is expensive O(N) where N = number of followed users

### Hybrid (Twitter's approach)
- Regular users: fan-out on write (push to followers' feeds)
- Celebrities (>X followers): fan-out on read (pull when needed)
- User follows celebrity: mark as "pull needed" in their feed

```python
def get_feed(user_id, page_token):
    # Get pre-computed feed (from fan-out-on-write users)
    feed = redis.zrevrange(f'feed:{user_id}', 0, 19)
    
    # Merge with celebrities' recent posts (fan-out-on-read)
    celebrity_ids = get_celebrities_followed(user_id)
    for celeb_id in celebrity_ids:
        recent_posts = db.get_recent_posts(celeb_id, limit=10)
        feed = merge_by_time(feed, recent_posts)
    
    return feed[:20]  # Return top 20
```

## Ride-Sharing System (Uber)

**Core challenges**: Location tracking, driver-rider matching, surge pricing, ETA calculation.

**Location tracking**:
```
- Drivers send GPS location every 4 seconds
- 1M active drivers = 250,000 location updates/sec
- Store in Redis with TTL (driver offline after 30s without update)
- Use geohashing to find nearby drivers
```

**Geohashing**:
Divides world into a grid. Each cell has a string key (precision by length). Nearby locations share long prefixes.

```python
import geohash

# Encode location
gh = geohash.encode(37.7749, -122.4194, precision=6)
# Returns: '9q8yy' (San Francisco area, ~1.2km x 0.6km cell)

# Find nearby drivers: query current geohash and 8 neighbors
neighbors = geohash.neighbors(gh)
all_cells = [gh] + neighbors
drivers = [redis.smembers(f'drivers:{cell}') for cell in all_cells]
```

**Matching algorithm**:
1. Find all available drivers within 2km (geohash query)
2. Filter by: vehicle type, acceptance rate, direction match
3. Select closest driver (Euclidean distance on coordinates)
4. Send request — driver has 30 seconds to accept
5. If rejected, try next closest driver

**Surge pricing**:
- Calculate demand/supply ratio per geohash cell per time window
- If ratio > threshold, apply multiplier
- Multiplier shown to rider before booking

## Design Framework Checklist

```
For every system design:
[ ] What's the scale? (QPS, DAU, data size)
[ ] What are the read/write patterns?
[ ] What consistency is required? (strong vs eventual)
[ ] What availability is required? (99.9% vs 99.999%)
[ ] What are the latency requirements? (p99 < 100ms?)
[ ] Where are the bottlenecks? (DB, network, compute)
[ ] How does it fail? (circuit breakers, retries, DLQ)
[ ] How does it scale? (horizontal, sharding, CDN)
[ ] How do you monitor it? (metrics, alerts, tracing)
```

quizBank.question: How would you generate unique short codes for a URL shortener?
quizBank.answer: Two main approaches: (1) Hash-based: MD5/SHA256 the long URL, take first 7 characters (base62). Problem: hash collisions and same URL produces same code (collision with different long URLs). (2) ID-based (preferred): Generate a unique integer ID (auto-increment DB, or distributed Snowflake ID), then encode in base62. 7 base62 characters = 62^7 = 3.5 trillion unique URLs. Advantages: no collisions, monotonically increasing (cache-friendly), no need to store long-to-short mapping lookup. For distributed ID generation, use Snowflake: 41 bits timestamp + 10 bits machine ID + 12 bits sequence = 64-bit unique ID.

quizBank.question: Should a URL shortener use 301 or 302 redirects?
quizBank.answer: 301 (Moved Permanently): Browser caches the redirect permanently. On subsequent visits, the browser goes directly to the long URL without hitting the shortener server. Pros: reduces server load. Cons: cannot track individual visits (analytics) or change the redirect later without cache invalidation. 302 (Found/Temporary): Browser re-requests the shortener on every visit. The shortener server sees every request. Pros: full analytics (click tracking, user agent, referrer), ability to update the target URL, A/B testing. Cons: server load is higher. Use 302 for analytics-enabled shorteners (bit.ly, tinyurl commercial). Use 301 for simple URL shorteners where analytics are not needed.

quizBank.question: How does WhatsApp handle message delivery when a recipient is offline?
quizBank.answer: When recipient is offline: (1) Sender's chat server sends message to recipient's designated chat server (routed by user ID). (2) Chat server checks Redis — user is offline. (3) Message is stored in Cassandra with status 'pending'. (4) Push notification is sent via APNS (iOS) or FCM (Android) to wake the app. (5) When recipient comes online and opens app, client fetches pending messages from server. (6) Once delivered and acknowledged by client, message status updates to 'delivered'. End-to-end encryption: messages encrypted on sender's device; server stores encrypted ciphertext and cannot read it.

quizBank.question: What is the celebrity problem in news feed systems and how is it solved?
quizBank.answer: The celebrity problem (also called the fan-out problem): If Justin Bieber (100M followers) posts a tweet and you use fan-out-on-write (pre-compute feed), you need to write to 100M users' feed caches per post. At 100M users x 500 bytes = 50GB of writes per tweet, instantly. Solution (Twitter's hybrid approach): (1) Regular users (< threshold followers): fan-out on write — write to followers' feed caches immediately. (2) Celebrities (> threshold): fan-out on read — do not pre-compute. (3) When reading feed: fetch pre-computed posts from regular users + pull latest posts from celebrities you follow, merge by timestamp.

quizBank.question: How does Uber match drivers to riders efficiently?
quizBank.answer: High-level matching flow: (1) Rider requests a ride. (2) System determines rider's geohash cell and queries nearby driver pool (current cell + 8 neighbors). (3) Filters available drivers by vehicle type, acceptance rate, direction. (4) Ranks candidates by estimated pickup time (considers distance and traffic). (5) Sends offer to best driver; driver has 30 seconds to accept. (6) On rejection, offer goes to next candidate. Geohashing: earth divided into a grid where each cell has a string key. Precision 6 = ~1.2km x 0.6km cells. Store active drivers as Redis sets per geohash cell (SADD drivers:9q8yy driver_id). Query 9 cells to get nearby drivers.

quizBank.question: How would you design a notification system for a mobile app with 500M users?
quizBank.answer: Components: (1) Notification Service — receives event (order shipped, like received) from upstream services via Kafka topic. (2) User Preference Service — checks if user has notifications enabled for this type. (3) Throttle/Rate Limiter — prevents spam (max N notifications/hour per user). (4) Fan-out Service — for broadcast notifications, expands to list of target users. (5) Delivery Service — calls APNs (iOS) or FCM (Android) with the notification payload. (6) Delivery Status Tracker — stores sent/delivered/opened status. DLQ for failed deliveries with retry. Key design: async throughout, idempotency on delivery (delivery token prevents duplicate sends).

quizBank.question: How would you design a distributed rate limiter?
quizBank.answer: Requirements: Limit API calls per user/API key across multiple servers. Components: (1) Token bucket in Redis — Lua script for atomic check-and-decrement of tokens per (user_id, window). (2) Sliding window log — store timestamps of recent requests; count in last N seconds. More accurate but higher memory. (3) Fixed window counter — INCR + EXPIRY per (user_id, minute). Simple but has edge case at window boundary (burst allowed at window transition). (4) Sliding window counter — hybrid: fixed window with partial count from previous window. Implementation: Redis Lua script for atomicity; cell headers (X-RateLimit-*); return 429 with Retry-After header on limit hit.

quizBank.question: How would you design Dropbox's file sync system?
quizBank.answer: Key components: (1) Client chunks files into 4MB blocks, computes SHA256 hash per block. (2) Upload Service: client checks which blocks are already uploaded (deduplication by hash). Only uploads new/changed blocks to S3. (3) Metadata Service: stores file tree, block list per file, version history in database. (4) Sync Service: when file changes, publishes event to Kafka. Other clients subscribed to that workspace receive event and download changed blocks. (5) Notification Service: long-polling or WebSocket for real-time sync. Deduplication: if two users upload the same file, only one copy stored in S3 (content-addressable storage by hash).

quizBank.question: How does YouTube handle video upload and transcoding?
quizBank.answer: Upload pipeline: (1) User uploads video to Upload Service via resumable upload (TUS protocol — supports pause/resume). (2) Raw video stored in S3/GCS. (3) Upload Service publishes message to Kafka: video_uploaded event with video_id, storage_path. (4) Transcoding Workers consume event, fetch raw video from storage, transcode into multiple formats and resolutions (360p, 720p, 1080p, 4K) and codecs (H.264, VP9, AV1). (5) Transcoded segments stored in CDN-origin storage. (6) Video Metadata Service marks video as available with all quality options. (7) CDN distributes segments globally. Adaptive bitrate (HLS/DASH): player selects quality based on network bandwidth.

quizBank.question: How would you design a typeahead/autocomplete system for a search bar?
quizBank.answer: Components: (1) Trie data structure stores all search terms. Each node stores a character; path from root to node forms prefix. Each node stores top-K most popular completions for that prefix. (2) Data pipeline: collect search queries, count frequency, top-K selection per prefix. Rebuild trie daily from aggregated data. (3) Trie stored in Redis or a dedicated service; replicated for high read throughput. (4) API: GET /autocomplete?q=uber returns top 10 suggestions for prefix 'uber'. Latency target: < 100ms. Scale: 5B queries/day = 58K QPS; with 10:1 read to write ratio, autocomplete gets ~580K QPS. Optimization: cache hot prefixes at CDN; debounce client requests (only send after 200ms pause in typing).

quizBank.question: What are the key differences between designing a system for 10K vs 10M vs 1B users?
quizBank.answer: 10K users: Single server, simple DB (PostgreSQL), no cache needed, basic monitoring. Vertical scaling works. 10M users: Multiple app servers + load balancer, read replicas, Redis cache (cache hit rate target >90%), CDN for static assets, basic sharding if write-heavy. 1B users: Multi-region active-active, database sharding (application-level or platform like Vitess/TiDB), purpose-built databases (Cassandra for high writes, Elasticsearch for search), sophisticated CDN strategy, circuit breakers, comprehensive observability, on-call rotations. The architecture changes fundamentally at each scale — 10M is not just '1000x 10K'.

quizBank.question: How would you approach designing Google Maps?
quizBank.answer: Core features: routing, search, live traffic. Key components: (1) Map data storage: vector tiles (Google's format) stored in S3, served via CDN. Tiles pre-rendered at multiple zoom levels. (2) Search: inverted index on place names + geospatial index (R-tree or S2 cells) for location queries. (3) Routing: road network as a weighted graph. Dijkstra/A* for routing. Precomputed highway routes with contraction hierarchies for long-distance routing. (4) Live traffic: aggregate GPS data from millions of devices (Kafka stream processing), compute average speed per road segment, update edge weights. (5) ETA: ML model trained on historical traffic patterns, current conditions, and route features. Scale: 1B+ users, map data is petabytes.

cheatSheet:
# Monitoring & Observability

## The Three Pillars

```mermaid
graph TB
    O[Observability] --> L[Logs]
    O --> M[Metrics]
    O --> T[Traces]
    L --> LD[What happened?\nStructured events]
    M --> MD[Is the system healthy?\nAggregated numbers]
    T --> TD[Why is it slow?\nRequest flow across services]
```

## Logs

### Structured Logging

```python
import structlog

log = structlog.get_logger()

# Bad: unstructured
logging.info(f"User {user_id} placed order {order_id} for ${amount}")
# Can't query: SELECT * WHERE user_id = 123

# Good: structured JSON
log.info("order.placed",
    user_id=user_id,
    order_id=order_id,
    amount=amount,
    trace_id=get_trace_id()  # Correlation with traces
)
# Queryable in Elasticsearch, CloudWatch Insights, Loki
```

### Log Levels
- **DEBUG**: Verbose developer info. Never in production by default (cost and noise).
- **INFO**: Normal operational events (user login, order placed).
- **WARN**: Unexpected but recoverable (retry succeeded, cache miss rate high).
- **ERROR**: Something failed, requires investigation.
- **CRITICAL/FATAL**: System-level failure, immediate action required.

### Log Aggregation Stack
```
Application -> Filebeat/Fluentd -> Elasticsearch -> Kibana
Application -> Fluentd -> Loki -> Grafana
AWS: CloudWatch Logs -> CloudWatch Insights
```

## Metrics

### Prometheus + Grafana

```python
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge

# Counter: monotonically increasing
http_requests = Counter('http_requests_total',
    'Total HTTP requests', ['method', 'endpoint', 'status'])

# Histogram: distribution of values (latency)
request_latency = Histogram('http_request_duration_seconds',
    'HTTP request latency', ['method', 'endpoint'],
    buckets=[.005, .01, .025, .05, .1, .25, .5, 1, 2.5, 5])

# Gauge: can go up or down
active_connections = Gauge('active_connections', 'Active DB connections')

# Usage:
with request_latency.labels(method='GET', endpoint='/api/orders').time():
    result = process_request()
http_requests.labels(method='GET', endpoint='/api/orders', status='200').inc()
```

### RED Method (Request-based services)
- **Rate**: Requests per second
- **Errors**: Error rate (4xx, 5xx)
- **Duration**: Response time (p50, p95, p99)

### USE Method (Resource-based)
- **Utilization**: % time resource is busy (CPU, disk)
- **Saturation**: Queue depth, waiting time
- **Errors**: Error count

### Key PromQL Queries

```promql
# Request rate (5-minute window)
rate(http_requests_total[5m])

# Error rate
rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])
/ rate(http_requests_total[5m])

# p99 latency
histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))

# Apdex score (satisfied < 100ms, tolerating < 400ms)
(sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.1"}[5m]))
 + sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.4"}[5m])) / 2)
/ sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_count[5m]))
```

## Distributed Tracing

### OpenTelemetry

```python
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

def process_order(order_id):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("process_order") as span:
        span.set_attribute("order.id", order_id)
        
        # Child spans automatically propagate trace context
        user = fetch_user(order_id)          # Child span
        payment = charge_payment(order_id)   # Child span
        
        span.set_attribute("payment.status", payment.status)
        return OrderResult(user, payment)
```

**Trace propagation**: W3C Trace Context header (traceparent) passes trace ID and span ID between services.

**Sampling**: Tracing every request is too expensive. Strategies:
- **Head-based**: Decide at request start (random 1% sample)
- **Tail-based**: Buffer spans, decide after seeing full trace (sample 100% of errors, 1% of successes)

### Jaeger / Zipkin / AWS X-Ray
Distributed tracing backends. Store spans, provide UI to visualize traces across services.

## SLIs, SLOs, SLAs

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| SLI (Service Level Indicator) | Metric measuring service quality | 99th percentile latency = 180ms |
| SLO (Service Level Objective) | Target for an SLI | p99 latency < 200ms, 99.9% of time |
| SLA (Service Level Agreement) | Contract with consequences | If availability < 99.9%, customer gets credit |

### Error Budgets

```
SLO: 99.9% availability
Allowed downtime/month: 0.1% x 30 days x 24 hours = 43.8 minutes/month

Error budget: 43.8 minutes

If error budget > 50% remaining: deploy freely, experiment
If error budget < 50% remaining: slow down, focus on reliability
If error budget exhausted: freeze deployments, focus on reliability
```

Error budgets align engineering incentives: reliability and feature velocity are balanced mathematically.

## Alerting Best Practices

### Alert on Symptoms, Not Causes
```
Bad: Alert on CPU > 80% (cause — doesn't always mean user impact)
Good: Alert on p99 latency > 500ms (symptom — user impact)
Good: Alert on error rate > 1% (symptom — user impact)
```

### Alert Tiers
- **Page (critical)**: Wakes someone at 3am. User-impacting, requires immediate action.
- **Ticket (warning)**: Creates a ticket. Needs attention in business hours.
- **Log (informational)**: Logged only. Useful for debugging, not actionable.

### Alerting Anti-Patterns
- **Alert fatigue**: Too many alerts that are not actionable. On-call engineers start ignoring alerts.
- **Flapping alerts**: Alert fires and clears repeatedly. Add hysteresis (require N consecutive breaches).
- **Missing runbooks**: Alert fires with no context on what to do.

## Incident Management

```
1. Detect: Alert fires
2. Acknowledge: On-call engineer picks up
3. Communicate: Set incident severity, notify stakeholders
4. Mitigate: Stop the bleeding (rollback, disable feature flag, increase capacity)
5. Resolve: Root cause fixed
6. Post-mortem: Blameless analysis of what happened and prevention
```

### Blameless Post-Mortem Template
```
Date: 2024-01-15
Severity: P1 (user-impacting)
Duration: 45 minutes
Impact: 15% of checkout requests failed

Timeline:
14:00 - Deploy of v2.3.1 begins
14:15 - Error rate rises to 15%
14:17 - Alert fires, on-call notified
14:22 - Rollback initiated
14:25 - Error rate returns to baseline

Root Cause: New version introduced N+1 query in checkout flow;
combined with traffic spike, exhausted DB connection pool.

Action Items:
- Add query count assertion to CI/CD pipeline
- Add DB connection pool saturation alert
- Load test checkout flow before each deploy
```

## Observability Stack Examples

**Open Source**: Prometheus + Grafana + Loki + Jaeger + OpenTelemetry
**AWS**: CloudWatch + X-Ray + CloudWatch Logs Insights
**Managed**: Datadog, New Relic, Honeycomb, Grafana Cloud

quizBank.question: What are the three pillars of observability and what question does each answer?
quizBank.answer: Logs: 'What happened?' — discrete events with context (user ID, request ID, error message). Structured logs enable querying. Best for debugging specific incidents. Metrics: 'Is the system healthy?' — aggregated numerical data over time (request rate, error rate, latency percentiles). Best for dashboards and alerting. Traces: 'Why is it slow?' — shows the path of a request across services, with timing for each segment. Best for identifying bottlenecks in distributed systems. All three together enable full observability: metrics alert you, logs help you investigate, traces show you exactly where time is spent.

quizBank.question: What are SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs? Give an example of each.
quizBank.answer: SLI (Service Level Indicator): A quantitative measure of service quality. Example: 'The fraction of HTTP requests that complete in under 200ms.' SLO (Service Level Objective): An internal target for an SLI. Example: 'p99 latency will be under 200ms, 99.9% of the time over a 30-day window.' This is what the engineering team commits to. SLA (Service Level Agreement): A contract with the customer with financial consequences for missing it. Example: 'If monthly availability is below 99.9%, customers receive a 10% service credit.' SLAs are typically less strict than SLOs (buffer for unexpected events).

quizBank.question: What is an error budget and how does it change team behavior?
quizBank.answer: Error budget is the maximum allowed unreliability implied by the SLO. Example: 99.9% availability SLO = 0.1% downtime budget = 43.8 minutes/month allowed downtime. The error budget bridges reliability and feature velocity. When error budget is healthy (>50% remaining): teams deploy freely, experiment, take risks. When error budget is low (<50%): teams slow down, focus on reliability. When exhausted: freeze non-critical deployments, 100% focus on reliability. This eliminates the adversarial relationship between SRE (reliability) and product (features) — both now share the same budget metric.

quizBank.question: What is the RED method and how does it differ from the USE method?
quizBank.answer: RED method (for request-based services like microservices): Rate (requests per second), Errors (error rate), Duration (latency distribution — p50, p95, p99). Answers: 'Is the service serving requests well?' USE method (for resource-based systems like servers, databases): Utilization (% time resource is busy), Saturation (how much work is queued), Errors (error rate). Answers: 'Are the resources being used efficiently?' Use RED for application services and APIs. Use USE for infrastructure (CPU, disk I/O, network). Brendan Gregg developed USE for systems; Tom Wilkie developed RED for services. Both are complementary.

quizBank.question: How does distributed tracing work and what is context propagation?
quizBank.answer: Distributed tracing follows a single request as it flows through multiple services. A trace consists of spans — each span represents a unit of work (one service call, one DB query). Each span has: trace_id (shared across all spans for a request), span_id (unique to this span), parent_span_id (links to the calling span), operation name, timing, and attributes. Context propagation: when service A calls service B, it attaches the trace context (trace_id, span_id) in HTTP headers (W3C traceparent header). Service B reads this, creates a child span with the same trace_id, and continues the trace. The tracing backend (Jaeger, Zipkin) assembles all spans into a visual trace.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between counter, gauge, and histogram in Prometheus?
quizBank.answer: Counter: Monotonically increasing value that only goes up (never decreases, except on restart). Examples: total requests, total errors, total bytes sent. Always use rate() in PromQL to compute per-second rate. Gauge: A value that can go up or down at any time. Examples: current active connections, memory usage, queue depth, temperature. Histogram: Samples observations and counts them in configurable buckets. Provides count, sum, and bucket counts. Used for latency distribution. Allows computing percentiles with histogram_quantile(). More accurate than summary for cross-service aggregation. Use counters + rate() for throughput, gauges for current state, histograms for latency/size distributions.

quizBank.question: What is alert fatigue and how do you prevent it?
quizBank.answer: Alert fatigue occurs when on-call engineers receive too many alerts, especially non-actionable ones. Effects: engineers start ignoring alerts (habituation), slow response times, missed critical incidents. Prevention: (1) Alert on symptoms (user impact), not causes (CPU high). (2) Every alert must be actionable — if on-call can't do anything about it, it should be a ticket or log, not a page. (3) Every alert must have a runbook. (4) Remove or demote alerts that don't require human intervention. (5) Add hysteresis — require N consecutive breaches before alerting. (6) Regular alert review meetings — audit and prune alerts quarterly.

quizBank.question: How would you debug a p99 latency spike in a microservices application?
quizBank.answer: Step 1: Check metrics — which service's p99 latency is elevated? RED dashboard by service. Step 2: Check for traffic spike — is QPS normal? If traffic spiked, resources may be saturated. Step 3: Check distributed traces — find representative slow traces. Look for which span is taking the longest. Step 4: Check resource saturation — CPU, memory, DB connections for the slow service. USE method metrics. Step 5: Check logs — ERROR logs in the slow service during the spike window. Step 6: Check dependencies — if the slow span is a DB call, check DB query latency, lock contention. If it's a downstream service call, repeat from Step 1 for that service. Trace + log correlation by trace_id is the fastest path to root cause.

quizBank.question: What is structured logging and why is it important?
quizBank.answer: Structured logging formats log entries as key-value pairs (usually JSON) instead of free-form text strings. Example unstructured: 'User 123 placed order 456 for $99.99 at 2024-01-15 14:30'. Example structured: {event: 'order.placed', user_id: 123, order_id: 456, amount: 99.99, timestamp: '2024-01-15T14:30:00Z', trace_id: 'abc123'}. Why it matters: (1) Machine-queryable — can filter by user_id=123 or amount>100 without regex. (2) Consistent schema — tools like Elasticsearch, CloudWatch Insights can index and search fields. (3) Easier alerting — alert when error_code='PAYMENT_FAILED' instead of matching regex patterns. Always include trace_id to correlate logs with distributed traces.

quizBank.question: What is a blameless post-mortem and why is 'blameless' important?
quizBank.answer: A post-mortem is a structured review after an incident to understand root causes and prevent recurrence. 'Blameless' means the focus is on systems and processes, not on punishing individuals who made mistakes. Why blameless matters: (1) If engineers fear punishment for mistakes, they hide problems and don't report near-misses. (2) Most incidents are caused by systemic issues (poor tooling, inadequate monitoring, unclear runbooks) not individual incompetence. (3) Psychological safety enables honest retrospectives. (4) Action items target systems: 'Add a test for this case' not 'Bob needs to be more careful'. Amazon and Google pioneered blameless post-mortems; they now produce better incident prevention than blame-based cultures.

quizBank.question: How do you handle high cardinality in Prometheus metrics?
quizBank.answer: Cardinality = number of unique label value combinations. High cardinality causes Prometheus to store millions of time series, consuming enormous memory and making queries slow. Problem example: labeling metrics with user_id (10M users = 10M time series). Rules: (1) Never use unbounded label values (user_id, request_id, URL path with IDs). (2) Labels should have bounded cardinality (HTTP method: GET/POST/PUT = 3 values; status code: 200/404/500 = ~10 values). (3) For high-cardinality analysis (per-user), use distributed tracing or logs instead. (4) If you must, use recording rules to pre-aggregate. (5) For cardinality analysis, use Prometheus's /api/v1/label/__name__/values endpoint or TSDB analyze command.

quizBank.question: How would you design the monitoring for a system processing financial transactions?
quizBank.answer: Critical metrics: (1) Transaction success rate (SLO: >99.99%). (2) Transaction latency p99 (SLO: <500ms). (3) Failed transactions by error type (insufficient funds, fraud blocked, timeout). (4) Payment processor availability (Stripe/PayPal API success rate). (5) Queue depth for async processing. Alerts: (1) Page immediately: success rate drops below 99.5%. (2) Page: p99 latency > 1 second. (3) Ticket: Payment processor error rate > 5%. Tracing: every transaction must be traceable end-to-end with transaction_id in all logs and spans. Audit logging: all transaction state changes logged to immutable store for compliance. Dashboards: real-time transaction volume, error breakdown by type, payment processor health.
